seo.title: LangGraph Guardrails for Legal
seo.description: Compliance guide for LangGraph agents in legal. Meet Attorney-client privilege protections, Bar association ethics rules requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Legal teams using LangGraph build complex, stateful agent workflows for tasks like contract review, eDiscovery, and case research, where cyclical execution and parallel branches handle multi-step legal reasoning. However, common failures like infinite loops, state corruption, and hallucinated citations can lead to malpractice, bar sanctions, or case dismissals as seen in Mata v. Avianca. Vex provides runtime verification to enforce attorney-client privilege, citation accuracy, and human review requirements.

complianceRequirements.description: Client confidential information must never be logged, stored, or transmitted without encryption and access controls
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex scans graph state and node outputs for PII/privileged data, blocks transmission, and enforces encryption policies before checkpointing

complianceRequirements.regulation: Bar association ethics rules (ABA Model Rule 1.1)
complianceRequirements.description: AI-generated legal content must be reviewed by licensed attorney before use in filings or advice
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex enforces human-in-the-loop breakpoints and blocks graph completion without attorney approval signatures

complianceRequirements.description: Citations must reference real, verifiable cases; hallucinated citations prohibited
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex validates case citations against legal databases in real-time, rejecting hallucinated references like 'Varghese v. China Southern Airlines'

complianceRequirements.description: Client data cannot be used for model training; all processing must be auditable
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex creates immutable audit trails of all graph executions and blocks data exfiltration to external training endpoints

industryRisks.name: Hallucinated case citations
industryRisks.description: LangGraph research nodes generate fake cases due to unverified LLM outputs merging in state
industryRisks.example: Mata v. Avianca (2023) - $45k sanction for AI-generated fake cases in federal filing

industryRisks.name: Privilege breach via checkpoint storage
industryRisks.description: Client confidential data persists in LangGraph checkpoints, accessible to unauthorized actors
industryRisks.example: Litigation firm exposes 500+ client matters through unencrypted state persistence

industryRisks.name: Bypassed human review
industryRisks.description: Conditional edges skip attorney breakpoints during cyclic execution
industryRisks.example: AI-drafted motion filed without review, containing substantive legal errors

industryRisks.name: State corruption in parallel discovery
industryRisks.description: Conflicting data from parallel eDiscovery branches corrupts case assessment state
industryRisks.example: Missed key documents due to merge conflicts, leading to adverse inferences at trial

implementation.title: Wrap graph.invoke() with Vex verification
implementation.description: Intercept all graph invocations to enforce citation validation and privilege scanning before execution completes
implementation.code:
import vex
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph

# Legal research graph
graph = StateGraph(...)

@vex.verify(
    rules=[
        "NO_PII: state contains no client names, SSNs, or case numbers",
        "VALID_CITATIONS: all citations reference real cases verifiable via Westlaw API",
        "HUMAN_REVIEW: attorney_signature in state"
    ]
)
def legal_research(input):
    result = graph.invoke(input)
    return result

# Usage
output = legal_research({"query": "ADA accommodation cases"})

implementation.title: Vex Node for real-time citation validation
implementation.description: Add Vex as a graph node to validate citations against legal databases during research workflows
implementation.code:
from langgraph.graph import END
from vex import Guard

async def vex_citation_check(state):
    guard = Guard.from_rules([
        "CITATION_EXISTS: state.citations exist in Westlaw/Pacer",
        "NO_HALLUCINATIONS: no fake cases like 'Varghese v. China Southern'"
    ])
    
    verified = await guard.check(state['research'])
    if not verified:
        return {"error": "Invalid citations detected", "blocked": True}
    
    return {"citations_verified": True}

# Add to graph
workflow.add_node("vex_check", vex_citation_check)
workflow.add_edge("research", "vex_check")
workflow.add_edge("vex_check", "attorney_review")

implementation.title: Enforce human-in-the-loop with Vex breakpoints
implementation.description: Prevent conditional edges from bypassing required attorney review using Vex state guards

caseStudyScenario.heading: LangGraph in Legal: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
A mid-sized litigation firm deploys LangGraph to streamline eDiscovery and motion drafting for an ADA class action. The graph orchestrates parallel branches: one analyzes 10,000+ documents for privilege, another researches case law, and a third drafts the opposition brief. Cyclical edges enable iterative refinement based on attorney feedback. The workflow processes client emails, deposition transcripts, and medical records while generating citations for summary judgment motions.

Without guardrails, the parallel privilege review branch flags 200 documents but state corruption during merge causes 50 privileged emails to be included in the draft motion. The research node hallucinates 'Smith v. Acme Corp (2024)' - a nonexistent case. Conditional edges bypass human review when confidence scores appear high, sending the flawed brief to opposing counsel.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Vex wrapping the graph.invoke() call immediately flags the privilege breach, blocking transmission of the 50 emails and creating an audit trail. The citation validation node queries Westlaw API, rejects the hallucinated 'Smith v. Acme Corp,' and forces research restart. Human-in-the-loop enforcement pauses execution, requiring attorney signature before motion drafting. The firm avoids a malpractice claim, discovers critical evidence in the recovered privileged documents, and wins summary judgment with verified citations.
